Keywords: diversity, cross-functional, perspective, skill-mix, cognitive-diversity, inclusion
Description: Assemble teams with diverse backgrounds, expertise, and thinking styles. Cognitive diversity produces better problem decomposition and more creative solutions.
When to Use: All complex problem-solving efforts; When the problem spans multiple domains; Innovation challenges; When prior teams have produced groupthink; Strategic initiatives
How to Facilitate: Identify required expertise domains; Include both specialists and generalists; Mix analytical and creative thinkers; Include people who will challenge assumptions; Ensure psychological safety for all voices
Signs of Dysfunction: Everyone agrees too quickly; Same solutions keep being proposed; Team lacks expertise in key areas; One perspective dominates; Minority voices go quiet
Remedy: Add external perspectives; Rotate team members; Bring in a provocateur; Use structured techniques (brainwriting) that ensure equal input; Assess cognitive diversity explicitly

Keywords: red-team, challenge, counter-argument, devils-advocate, adversarial, stress-test
Description: Assign a dedicated person or sub-team to attack the prevailing recommendation. Their job is to find flaws, present counterarguments, and stress-test assumptions.
When to Use: Before finalizing recommendations; When stakes are high; When team is converging too quickly; When dealing with irreversible decisions; Strategy reviews; Risk assessment
How to Facilitate: Formally assign the role (not volunteer); Rotate the role to prevent burnout; Protect the red team from social punishment; Give them access to all data; Present red team findings formally
Signs of Dysfunction: Team resents the red teamer; Red team is token/toothless; Leadership dismisses challenges; Red team attacks people not ideas; No formal process for incorporating red team feedback
Remedy: Make it a valued formal role; Leadership must model openness to challenge; Red team reports to decision maker directly; Celebrate good challenges; Follow up on red team findings

Keywords: brainstorm, ideation, creative, divergent, brainwriting, nominal-group, silent
Description: Use structured techniques to generate ideas without social dynamics killing creativity. Silent individual generation before group discussion produces more and better ideas.
When to Use: Decomposition phase; Solution generation; Creative problem solving; When team needs fresh ideas; When extroverts dominate; When hierarchy suppresses junior voices
How to Facilitate: Silent individual idea generation (5-10 min); Share ideas anonymously or round-robin; Build on others ideas; Defer judgment during generation; Separate divergent (generate) from convergent (evaluate) phases
Signs of Dysfunction: Loudest person dominates; Ideas are criticized immediately; Hierarchy determines whose ideas survive; Same ideas keep appearing; Introverts dont contribute
Remedy: Always start with silent individual generation; Use anonymous posting; Enforce no-criticism during brainstorming; Use round-robin sharing; Give equal time to each person

Keywords: workshop, session, facilitation, agenda, timeboxing, structure, meeting-design
Description: Design working sessions with clear structure: pre-work, timed activities, and concrete outputs. Prevent meetings from being discussions without decisions.
When to Use: Every team working session; When you need to produce output not just discuss; Sprint planning; Analysis reviews; Synthesis workshops; Decision meetings
How to Facilitate: Define desired output before the meeting; Send pre-read materials; Design timed activities; Assign a facilitator (not the decision maker); End with decisions and action items; Document outcomes immediately
Signs of Dysfunction: Meetings run over time; No clear output; Same discussion repeats; People come unprepared; No decisions made; Action items unclear
Remedy: Strict timeboxing; Mandatory pre-read; Clear facilitator role; Written output required; Parking lot for off-topic items; Action items with owners and dates before leaving

Keywords: hypothesis, test, validate, iterate, evidence-based, update, sprint
Description: Team operates in hypothesis-test-update cycles. Start each sprint with a hypothesis about the answer. Design analyses to test it. Update based on evidence. Prevents aimless research.
When to Use: Throughout the problem-solving process; After initial decomposition; During analysis phase; Weekly team syncs; When team needs focus and direction
How to Facilitate: State team hypothesis explicitly at start of each cycle; Assign analyses to test specific parts; Daily/weekly check-ins: what did we learn? Does hypothesis still hold? What should we test next?; Track confidence level over time
Signs of Dysfunction: Team is gathering data without clear purpose; Analyses dont connect to hypotheses; Team is afraid to state a hypothesis; No one updates the hypothesis when evidence changes; Data collection becomes an end in itself
Remedy: Force a Day 1 answer; Write down the hypothesis visibly; Link every analysis to the hypothesis directly; Track and display confidence level; Celebrate hypothesis changes as learning not failure

Keywords: workplan, timeline, accountability, tracking, milestone, deadline, progress
Description: Maintain a living workplan that maps analyses to issues, assigns ownership, sets deadlines, and tracks progress. The workplan is the teams navigation system.
When to Use: After prioritization; Throughout analysis phase; When managing multiple parallel analyses; When team members work independently; To prevent scope creep
How to Facilitate: Create workplan matrix: Issue > Hypothesis > Analysis > Data > Owner > Deadline > Status; Review in every team meeting; Update as priorities shift; Ensure no analysis is orphaned (no owner) or aimless (no hypothesis)
Signs of Dysfunction: Analyses are disconnected from issues; No one knows who is doing what; Deadlines slip without discussion; New analyses appear without rationale; Team meeting is just status update not problem solving
Remedy: Visualize the workplan (shared board/doc); Start every meeting with workplan review; Require new analyses to be justified against an issue; Weekly priority re-ranking; Kill stale analyses explicitly

Keywords: conflict, debate, productive-disagreement, psychological-safety, candor, challenge
Description: Encourage vigorous debate about ideas while maintaining respect for people. The best teams fight hard about ideas and leave the room as allies.
When to Use: All team discussions; Decision meetings; Analysis reviews; When disagreement is present (channeling it productively); When team is too harmonious (may be suppressing dissent)
How to Facilitate: Establish ground rules: attack ideas not people; Ask for counterarguments explicitly; Reward disagreement and challenge; Leader speaks last; Use structured debate format when topics are contentious
Signs of Dysfunction: Personal attacks; Avoidance of disagreement; Behind-the-scenes lobbying; Same people always winning debates; Disagreement leads to lasting resentment
Remedy: Set explicit norms; Practice separating person from position; Use 'steel man' (strengthen the opposing argument before countering); Leader models vulnerability; Process disagreements formally

Keywords: checkpoint, demo, share, feedback, iteration, early, course-correct
Description: Share work-in-progress frequently rather than waiting for perfection. Early sharing catches wrong directions, surfaces new insights, and maintains team alignment.
When to Use: Throughout analysis phase; When team members work independently; When the problem is evolving; When stakeholder expectations need managing; When direction uncertainty is high
How to Facilitate: Daily or bi-daily quick syncs; Weekly deeper reviews; Share analysis drafts not polished outputs; Invite constructive criticism early; Use 'one-minute paper' format: what I found, what it means, what I need
Signs of Dysfunction: Work is shared only when 'done'; Team members are surprised by each others findings; Direction diverges between sub-teams; Late-stage rework because of missed context; Stakeholders learn results only at the end
Remedy: Lower the bar for sharing; Celebrate early ugly drafts; Use visual progress boards; Structure quick syncs (2 min per person max); Create safe space for incomplete thinking

Keywords: safety, trust, vulnerability, risk-taking, learning, mistakes-ok, speak-up
Description: Team members feel safe to take risks, admit mistakes, ask questions, and challenge ideas without fear of punishment or embarrassment. Foundation for all effective team problem solving.
When to Use: Must be established from the start; Essential for all other team patterns; Especially important for diverse teams; When tackling novel problems; When failure is possible and learning is critical
How to Facilitate: Leader demonstrates vulnerability first; Respond positively to questions and challenges; Treat mistakes as learning; Avoid blame; Explicitly invite disagreement; Follow through when someone raises a concern
Signs of Dysfunction: People stay silent in meetings but complain afterward; No one asks questions; Mistakes are hidden; Ideas come only from senior people; Team avoids difficult topics; People hedge everything
Remedy: Leader admits own mistakes publicly; Thank people for raising concerns; Act on feedback; Remove people who punish others for speaking up; Run anonymous feedback sessions; Measure and track psychological safety

Keywords: rotation, ownership, development, learning, fresh-eyes, cross-training
Description: Rotate who leads different parts of the analysis. Fresh eyes catch blind spots. Cross-training builds team capability. Prevents silos and key-person dependencies.
When to Use: Multi-week problem-solving efforts; When one person has been stuck; Team development; When you want to build bench strength; When analysis has stalled
How to Facilitate: Assign different issue owners for different phases; Have owners present each others findings; Pair junior and senior on complex analyses; Rotate facilitation role; Debrief each rotation for learning
Signs of Dysfunction: Same person always leads; Team has single points of failure; Junior members never present; Owners become territorial; Knowledge is siloed in individuals
Remedy: Formal rotation schedule; Paired ownership model; Mandatory handoff documentation; Growth objectives tied to rotation; Celebrate cross-functional learning
